description:
## Sachverhalt

End-to-end Prod-Test über `searxng-cli search_batch` (3 Queries, 60 Slots) am 2026-05-06 hat drei strip_bloat-Lücken aufgedeckt. Alle drei sichtbar im Output von `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/pipeline_smoke_*.md` und in den Live-CLI-Results.

### 1. Read-more Regex-Bug (Hauptfinding, 3/3 Queries betroffen)

**Datei:** `src/search/snippet.py` `_strip_bloat`, Zeile mit `re.sub(r'\bRead more\b.*', '', text)`.

**Problem:** Das `\b` nach `more` verlangt einen Wort-Grenzübergang (transition zwischen `\w` und `\W`). Google's SERP-Format konkateniert "Read more" oft direkt mit dem nächsten Wort oder einer Klammer ohne Whitespace, z.B. `Read moreBest...`, `Read more5,0(5)...`, `Read moreWhat...`. Da steht `e` (word) gefolgt von `B`/`5`/`W` (alle word) — kein Grenzübergang, regex matched nicht.

**Konkrete Treffer aus dem CLI-Test:**
- Q1 Pos 6 (Reddit r/rust State of Async): `...you can create a large amount ...Read moreBest Async Runtime for HTTP/Networking? : r/rust31 posts17 Jun 2024Why does Rust not have a standard async runtime?83 posts4 May 2022More results from www.reddit.com`
- Q2 Pos 2 (IBM What is an attention mechanism): `...most relevant parts of input data.Read moreWhat is an attention mechanism?Why are attention mechanisms...`
- Q3 Pos 4 (umzug.de Sonderkündigungsrecht): `...Per E-Mail oder Fax ist die Kündigung nicht möglich. Da es ...Read more5,0(5) More information about the review ratings.Reviews aren't verified by Google Search`

**Fix:** `r'\bRead more\b.*'` → `r'\bRead more.*'` (trailing `\b` raus). Trailing `\b` ist semantisch unnötig — wir wollen alles AB `Read more` strippen, egal was direkt danach kommt.

**Test:** 3 Test-Strings (siehe Q1/Q2/Q3 oben), strip_bloat sollte nach `Read more` abschneiden in allen Fällen. Plus eine Negativ-Probe mit "Read more about Y" (Space + Wort danach) — die sollte unverändert bleiben weil das Pattern weiterhin matchen muss.

### 2. Translate-this-page Prefix (sekundär, 2 Treffer in Q3)

**Problem:** Foreign-Language-Results von Google haben oft Prefix `· Translate this page` am Anfang des Snippets. Aktuell nicht in strip_bloat.

**Konkrete Treffer:**
- Q3 Pos 1 (umziehen.de): `· Translate this pageÜber ihr Sonderkündigungsrecht...`
- Q3 Pos 4 (umzug.de): `· Translate this pageDie Sonderkündigung der Wohnung...`

**Fix:** Neue Zeile in `_strip_bloat`: `text = re.sub(r'^· Translate this page', '', text)` (oder `^\s*·\s*Translate this page` falls führende Whitespaces vorkommen).

### 3. Emoji Strip (optional, 1 Treffer)

**Problem:** Third-Party-Content kann Emojis enthalten die als Snippet-Lärm wirken.

**Konkreter Treffer:**
- Q2 Pos 9 (towardsai.net): `Author (s): Vivek Tiwari Table of Contents Introduction 🚀 Background: The Evolution of Sequence Models 🌿 Transformer: A High-Level Overview 🌐 The Attention Mechanism Explained 🔍 Self-Attention in Detail 📚 Multi-Head Attention 🎯 Positional Encoding 📍 Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks 🔄 Implementation Details 🛠️ Code Examples 💻 Results and Performance 📈 ...`

**Fix:** Optionaler Emoji-Strip in `_strip_bloat`:
```python
text = re.sub(r'[\U0001F300-\U0001FAFF\U00002600-\U000027BF\U0001F000-\U0001F2FF\U0001F600-\U0001F64F]', '', text)
```

Lower priority — User-Mehrwert ist klein, third-party content ist selten so emoji-spammig.

## Scope

`src/search/snippet.py` `_strip_bloat`. Auch in `dev/search_pipeline/_lib/text.py` `strip_bloat` parallel updaten — beides hat heute identische Regex-Logik per Refactor-Centralisierung.

3 Patches: Patch 1 (Read-more) ist required, Patch 2 (Translate-this-page) sinnvoll, Patch 3 (Emoji) optional. Alle in einem Worker-Cycle.

## Verifikation

Nach Fix: `searxng-cli search_batch <3 queries>` rerun, Q1/Q2/Q3-Treffer von oben prüfen — alle "Read more"-Tails weg, kein "· Translate this page" Prefix in deutschen Results, ggf. keine Emojis im towardsai-Snippet (falls Patch 3 angewendet).

## APPROACH

Worker spawn auf dev. _strip_bloat in beiden Files (src + _lib) updaten, optional Unit-Tests pro Patch (kleine Test-Strings, expected output). Smoke-Run mit denselben 3 Queries, Spot-Check der konkreten Pos-Treffer. Commit + merge.
title: strip_bloat: Read-more no-boundary fix + Translate-this-page strip + optional emoji strip

description:
Recherche aus Session 2026-03-15 umsetzen. RAG-Stack (Qwen3-8B Embeddings, SPLADE, Qwen3-8B Reranker, pgvector, RRF Fusion) ist funktional. Naechster Schritt: Retrieval-Qualitaet verbessern.

Recherchierte Verbesserungen (SearXNG Websuche + RAG Docs):
1. Contextual Retrieval (Anthropic) — LLM-generierter Kontext-Prefix pro Chunk vor Embedding
2. HyDE — Hypothetical Document Embeddings als Query-Expansion
3. Parent-Child Chunking — Kleine Chunks (100-300 tokens) fuer Retrieval, grosse (800-2000) fuer Context
4. ColBERT Late Interaction — pgvector hat experimentellen Support, 30-50% besserer Recall
5. pgvector HNSW Tuning — ef_search, iterative scanning (pgvector 0.8)
6. Chunk-Size Tuning — Vecta Benchmark: recursive 512-token = 69% Accuracy (Platz 1), wir nutzen 1000 chars

Quellen gespeichert in Searching Suite Reports (01_reports/search_report_20260315_020828.md).

description:
DuckDuckGo als zweite General-Search-Engine im Stack. Browser/pydoll Pattern (Konsistenz mit Google), html.duckduckgo.com Endpoint (proven SearXNG path), 4 req/min uniformer Rate-Limit (Google-Baseline).

## Why

Diversifizierung weg vom reinen Google-Index als General-Source. DDG liefert eigenen Index-Mix (Bing + eigener Crawler), andere Snippet-Logik. Coverage durch Engine-Diversität, nicht durch Google-Volume — mvz-Strategie ("in die Breite").

API-basierte Engines (HN-Algolia 7ue, Marginalia jmo) waren in vorherigen Iterationen limitiert (Rate-Limits, paid keys). DDG ist scrape-basiert, kein API-Key, in SearXNG seit Jahren production-tested.

## What

- Neue Engine `src/search/engines/duckduckgo.py` (BaseEngine subclass, mirror google.py Pattern)
- Smoke-Script `dev/search_pipeline/02_ddg_smoke.py` (analog 01_google_smoke.py, self-contained)
- Config-Block in `dev/search_pipeline/config.yml` (`duckduckgo:` neben `google:`)
- Routing in `src/search/search_web.py` (Import + ENGINES dict entry)
- Pre-register Rate-Limiter `_limiters["duckduckgo"]` mit max_requests=4, window_seconds=60
- DOCS-Updates: src/search/DOCS.md, dev/search_pipeline/DOCS.md, CLAUDE.md
- sources/sources.md: SearXNG duckduckgo.py reference

## Where

- src/search/engines/duckduckgo.py (NEW, mirror google.py struktur)
- src/search/search_web.py (+2 lines: import + ENGINES dict)
- dev/search_pipeline/02_ddg_smoke.py (NEW, mirror 01_google_smoke.py)
- dev/search_pipeline/config.yml (+ duckduckgo block)
- cli.py (help text update wenn applicable)
- decisions/ (Engine-relevante decision-File: aktualisieren oder neu anlegen — Worker entscheidet basierend auf state)

## Endpoint Decision

`https://html.duckduckgo.com/html/?q=<encoded>&kl=wt-wt`

via pydoll Browser-Tab. Begründung:
- SearXNG benutzt diesen Endpoint, nicht den JS-SERP von duckduckgo.com
- Selectors aus SearXNG verfügbar (siehe unten)
- Server-rendered HTML, keine JS-Result-Loading-Mechanik nötig
- Browser-Pool-Konsistenz mit Google bleibt erhalten

## DDG Selectors (aus SearXNG xpath, nach CSS übersetzt)

- Result block: `div#links > div.web-result`
- Title link: `h2 a` (innerhalb result-block)
- URL: `h2 a` href attribute
- Snippet: `a.result__snippet`
- CAPTCHA-Detection: `form#challenge-form` anywhere in body

Worker MUST verify diese Selectors gegen aktuelle DDG html-page in Phase A (DOM-Probe).

## Required Headers (laut SearXNG für DDG bot-blocker)

- Static User-Agent (Mac Chrome, schon in config.yml unter browser.user_agent)
- Sec-Fetch-Dest: document
- Sec-Fetch-Mode: navigate
- Sec-Fetch-Site: same-origin
- Sec-Fetch-User: ?1
- Referer: https://html.duckduckgo.com/
- Accept-Language: en,en-EN;q=0.7
- Cookie: kl=wt-wt

Worker prüft in Phase A ob pydoll's `tab.go_to()` diese Headers default mitbringt oder per CDP injected werden müssen.

## Reference Files

- `src/search/engines/google.py` (BaseEngine subclass pattern, _limiters preregister)
- `dev/search_pipeline/01_google_smoke.py` (smoke harness pattern, self-contained)
- `dev/search_pipeline/config.yml` (config block pattern, google: section)
- SearXNG `searx/engines/duckduckgo.py` auf GitHub (URL/Headers/Selectors source of truth — bereits gelesen, siehe Bead-Description)

## Out of Scope

- Pagination (vqd-Token-Mechanik) — nur Page 1, vqd kommt später wenn nötig
- Time-Range / Region selection (kl=wt-wt fix für jetzt)
- Stack-Exchange engine (separater Bead civ)
- Marginalia (separater Bead jmo)
- Bing-Repair (separat)
- Preview-Feature (Bead j0r — separater Worker in dieser Session, nach DDG-Konvergenz)

## KPI

- 02_ddg_smoke.py liefert 30/30 OK (oder dokumentierter non-OK Breakdown für DDG-spezifische Eigenheiten)
- Live CLI test: `searxng-cli search_web "<query>" --engines duckduckgo` liefert ≥5 Hits mit url/title/snippet
- Smoke-Report im selben Format wie Google-Smoke (`dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/`)
title: searxng: DuckDuckGo engine implementation (browser/pydoll on html-endpoint)

description:
## Why

Bead searxng-hc3 fixed `html.unescape` on the OpenAlex abstract path (`_reconstruct_abstract`) and the preview fetcher (`_fetch_one`). v1 pipeline smoke (dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/pipeline_smoke_20260505_180433.md) shows 4 surviving entity remnants — all `&` patterns — in code paths hc3 did NOT cover:

- Line 1085: OpenAlex title `... (RLHF)&nbsp;` 
- Line 2121: title `Datenschutzerklärung, Impressum & Co.` (likely OpenAlex or CrossRef)
- Line 2240: CrossRef synthesis snippet `(2026), International Research Journal of Modernization in Engineering Technology & Science` — the synthesized `Author, Year, Container-Title` fallback, where `container-title[0]` is not unescaped
- Line 3057: title `Cloud & Edge Systems` (likely OpenAlex)

## Scope

Extend `html.unescape` to title fields and CrossRef synthesis components. Same pattern as hc3, leaf-level fix.

### Fix sites

1. **`src/search/engines/openalex.py._parse_results`** — Title field at `work.get("title")` is not unescaped. Wrap. Add `import html` if not already imported (it should be, hc3 added it for the abstract path).

2. **`src/search/engines/crossref.py._parse_results`** — Title field is not unescaped. Wrap. Plus the synthesis fallback (when abstract is absent or whitespace-only): the `container-title[0]` component must be unescaped before joining into the synthesized string. The author surname can in principle also have entities — wrap defensively. Confirm `import html` is present (DOCS suggests crossref already uses it for abstract regex strip — if so, just add to the title + synthesis paths).

3. **Spot-check other engines for parallel gaps:**
   - `google.py` / `scholar.py` — pydoll-extracted DOM text, generally not entity-encoded by the time it reaches us, but verify.
   - `duckduckgo.py` / `mojeek.py` — same reasoning.
   - `lobsters.py` — same.
   - `stack_exchange.py` — already does `html.unescape` on titles per DOCS.

If other engines have title fields that pass entity-encoded text through, fix them with the same pattern. If not, no change needed there.

## Verification

Re-run the smoke (using the current `dev/search_pipeline/11_pipeline_smoke.py`):

```
./venv/bin/python3 dev/search_pipeline/11_pipeline_smoke.py --max-queries 30 > /tmp/entity_extend_run.log 2>&1
```

Grep the new report for entity remnants:

```
grep -cE "&#[0-9]+;|&[a-z]+;" <new_report_path>
```

Must return 0. If non-zero, list the surviving cases and identify the code path.

## Out of scope

- The hc3 fix itself (already merged)
- The plugin-routing handling (deliberately NOT changing — search-side stays plugin-agnostic per user decision)
- The slot-allocation redesign (separate concern, dl9 v3 worker)
- The snippet-bloat issue in Q1 (separate diagnosis, depends on snippet_source field being added by dl9 v3)

## Dependencies

- None blocking. Touches `src/search/engines/`, disjoint from dl9 v3 worker (which touches `src/search/search_web.py`, `cache.py`, `cli.py`).
- Will be merged on dev. Final dev state after both merges: v3 architecture + entity decoding completed.

## Related

- searxng-hc3 (closed) — original entity-fix on abstract + preview paths
- searxng-dl9 (in flight) — pipeline smoke + slot redesign
title: Entity decoding extension — engine titles + CrossRef synthesis (hc3 follow-up)

close_reason: Telemetry leak confirmed as smoke-script artifact (01_dual_mode_smoke.py:140 captures subprocess stdout incl. Crawl4AI's [FETCH]/[SCRAPE]/[COMPLETE] progress messages). Prod MCP scrape_url_workflow returns result[0].text directly (no subprocess), 02_raw_smoke.py uses result.markdown.raw_markdown directly via Python (no subprocess). Both prod paths confirmed leak-free this session. No prod code change needed.
description:
## Problem

Every successful Mode 2 (`scrape_url`) output starts with 6 lines of Crawl4AI's stderr/stdout progress telemetry that should never reach the user:

\`\`\`
[FETCH]... ↓ <url>
| ✓ | ⏱: 1.67s
[SCRAPE].. ◆ <url>
| ✓ | ⏱: 0.14s
[COMPLETE] ● <url>
| ✓ | ⏱: 1.82s
\`\`\`

Evidence: \`dev/scrape_pipeline/01_dual_mode_outputs/20260505_185247/mode2_filtered/\*.md\` — every one of the 18 successful M2 files has this 6-line prefix. ~108 lines of pure noise across the 18-URL test set.

## Root cause (suspected)

Crawl4AI logs progress telemetry to stdout/stderr that gets captured into the wrapper's output. The \`scrape_url\` CLI subcommand prints \`result[0].text\` which apparently includes this prefix.

## Fix

In \`src/scraper/scrape_url.py\` (\`scrape_url_workflow\` or wherever the markdown is assembled into the \`TextContent\`): regex-strip Crawl4AI's progress patterns before constructing the output text. Patterns to strip:

- Lines starting with \`[FETCH]...\`, \`[SCRAPE]..\`, \`[COMPLETE]\`
- The \`| ✓ | ⏱: ...\` continuation lines

Spot-check \`scrape_url_raw\` too — likely has the same leak in the raw markdown.

## Verification

After fix, re-run any 1-2 URLs through \`searxng-cli scrape_url <url>\` and confirm the output starts with \`# Content from: ...\` directly, no \`[FETCH]\` prefix.

## Out of scope

- The PruningFilter quality issues (Sphinx chrome, table corruption) — separate concerns under bead searxng-oft
- Library swap question — independent

## Why this is its own bead

Surfaced during scrape-prep Step 3 dual-mode hands-on (bead searxng-oft Step 3 comment). Independent of any library swap or filter tuning. Pure wrapper-level fix. Surfaced as a finding, doesn't fit the architectural redesign scope, gets its own quick-fix bead.
title: Strip Crawl4AI progress telemetry from scrape_url output (wrapper noise leak)

close_reason: Crawling Performance geloest: Prefetch Mode (49x Speedup), arun_many + SemaphoreDispatcher (parallel crawl, concurrency=10), 3-Stufen-Kaskade Sitemap→Prefetch→BFS in crawl_site.py. Apple Metal Docs 521 Seiten jetzt in Minuten statt 30+.
description:
Apple Metal Docs (521 Seiten) braucht 30+ Minuten mit --no-prefetch (serielles BFS, ~2s/Seite). sbert.net Exploration mit 500 max_pages haengt ebenfalls. Prefetch ist 49x schneller aber funktioniert nicht fuer SPA Sites.

Optionen:
1. arun_many auch fuer --no-prefetch: BFS Discovery mit vollem Rendering (seriell), dann Content-Crawl parallel — aber Discovery ist der Bottleneck
2. Streaming BFS: Ergebnisse sofort verarbeiten statt auf alle warten
3. Sitemap-Detection: robots.txt/sitemap.xml parsen statt BFS Discovery — fuer grosse Docs Sites oft vorhanden
4. Concurrency in BFS selbst: Crawl4AI hat keinen nativen Parameter dafuer

Repo: searxng. Key Files: crawl_site.py, src/scraper/explore_site.py
title: Crawling Performance: Serial BFS viel zu langsam fuer grosse Sites

close_reason: Thema gehört ins RAG-Projekt (llama-server Config), nicht SearXNG. Kein Fortschritt, keine Kommentare. Bei Bedarf im RAG-Repo neu aufmachen mit Optionen: -ngl reduzieren, kleineres Embedding-Modell, ubatch-size reduzieren.
description:
Problem: llama-server (Qwen3-Embedding-8B-Q8, -ngl 99) belegt 8.2GB unified memory und saturiert Memory Bandwidth beim Batch-Indexing. System wird unresponsive (Ghostty, Browser, Claude konkurrieren um gleichen Speicher-Bus).

Optionen die geklaert werden muessen:
1. -ngl reduzieren (weniger GPU Layers) — Trade-off Speed vs Responsiveness unbekannt
2. Kleineres Embedding-Modell (1-2B statt 8B) — Quality Trade-off unbekannt
3. ubatch-size reduzieren — Auswirkung auf Bandbreite-Contention unbekannt

Quellen die VOR einer Entscheidung konsultiert werden muessen:
- Crawl4AI Docs (RAG Collection) — Embedding-Modell Empfehlungen
- llama.cpp Docs/Issues — Metal Memory Bandwidth, -ngl Auswirkung auf Apple Silicon
- Qwen3 Embedding Benchmarks — Qualitaetsvergleich 8B vs kleinere Varianten
- Apple Silicon Memory Architecture — Unified Memory Bandwidth Sharing

Kontext: Reranker-Server (6.7GB) wurde waehrend Indexing gestoppt — das hilft, loest aber nicht das Grundproblem.

Repo: searxng (MCP Plugin) + RAG (Embedding Server Config)
Files: RAG/start.sh (Server-Start-Config), RAG/workflow.py (Indexing Loop)
title: GPU Throttling: Embedding Server Resource Management beim Indexing

description:
## What

Follow-up to the current Crawl4AI_Docs RAG indexing run (60 URLs from docs.crawl4ai.com sitemap, worker `crawl4ai-index` 2026-05-06). Two additional steps to round out the collection's coverage of the Crawl4AI library, to be done in the next test/walkthrough session.

## Pre-condition

The current 60-URL indexing run completed successfully and is verifiable via `rag-cli search --query "..." --collection Crawl4AI_Docs`. If the first run failed or produced poor retrieval quality, that needs to be fixed BEFORE this bead's items are useful — re-investigate the first run, then come back here.

## Step 1 — Add CHANGELOG.md + README.md via gh-cli

The mkdocs nav explicitly links the Crawl4AI changelog as an external GitHub URL (not mirrored into docs.crawl4ai.com). The README has the architecture overview and feature list in compact form, complementing the distributed coverage in the /core/ doc pages.

- Activate `github-search` skill (`Skill(skill="github-search")`) for gh-cli reference
- Fetch raw markdown via `gh-cli get_file_content unclecode crawl4ai CHANGELOG.md` and `gh-cli get_file_content unclecode crawl4ai README.md`
- Write both to `~/Documents/ai/Meta/ClaudeCode/MCP/RAG/data/documents/Crawl4AI_Docs/` as plain .md files (skip MinerU — they're already markdown)
- Re-index the collection (incremental, append-mode if rag-cli supports it; otherwise full re-index of the directory)

## Step 2 — Recon crawl4ai.dev (third-party guides)

`crawl4ai.dev` is a SEPARATE domain from `docs.crawl4ai.com`. Tagline: "Developer-focused Crawl4AI guides with real configs, proven patterns, and comparisons. Build AI agent data extraction and automation with practical tutorials."

- `searxng-cli explore_site https://crawl4ai.dev --max-pages 100`
- Spot-check 2-3 pages with `searxng-cli scrape_url` to evaluate substance (real recipes vs. marketing rehash)
- Decision tree:
  - Substantive unique content → index as SEPARATE collection `Crawl4AI_Guides_External` (don't pollute the official docs collection with third-party content)
  - Marketing rehash → skip, document in this bead's close reason

## URL categories EXPLICITLY rejected during 2026-05-06 review (do NOT re-investigate)

| Category | Examples | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| DeepWiki auto-doc | deepwiki.com/unclecode/crawl4ai | Redundant — auto-generated from same source tree as official docs |
| Tutorial blogs | scrapfly.io, scrapingbee, pondhouse-data, dev.to/ali_dz, rumjahn | Medium-quality reformulations — official docs cover the same better |
| Comparison posts | firecrawl.dev/alternatives, dataresearchtools, scrapeops, prospeo | Biased marketing or listicle slop, low signal-to-noise |
| LangChain integration tutorial | medium.com/@kpetropavlov | Different architectural use case — searxng uses Crawl4AI direct, not as LangChain tool |
| PyPI page | pypi.org/project/Crawl4AI/ | README + install snippet — already covered by Step 1 |
| Academic papers | CAMEL-AI Zenodo, "Cooperative Content Negotiation", "Building Effective AI Coding Agents" | Out of scope for Crawl4AI_Docs collection — would belong in a separate `Web_Scraping_Research` or `Agentic_AI_Architecture` collection if at all |
| Stack Overflow Q&A | LLMConfig, login handling | Veraltet schnell, blähen RAG mit Spezialfällen — better to fetch on-the-fly via search_web when the specific problem hits |

## Closing condition

Both steps complete (or Step 2 explicitly skipped after recon with documented reason), `rag-cli search` against the extended collection returns relevant hits for queries like "crawl4ai changelog v0.8", "crawl4ai architecture overview", or — if Step 2 ran — "crawl4ai recipe MCP server".

## Approach for next session

Phase A: Verify first run still good — `rag-cli search --collection Crawl4AI_Docs --query "adaptive crawling"` should return useful chunks. If bad → fix before proceeding.
Phase B: Step 1 (CHANGELOG + README via gh-cli, incremental index).
Phase C: Step 2 (crawl4ai.dev explore + evaluate + decide).
Phase D: Close this bead with summary of what was done, what was skipped and why.
title: Crawl4AI_Docs RAG: extend with CHANGELOG/README + crawl4ai.dev recon (next test run)

description:
First plattform-Engine im Stack: HackerNews via Algolia Search API. Pure HTTP analog zu CrossRef, kein Browser, keine Konkurrenz mit Google um Tabs.

## What

- Neue Engine `src/search/engines/hn.py` (httpx-basiert, kein pydoll)
- Smoke-Script `dev/search_pipeline/03_hn_smoke.py` analog zu `01_google_smoke.py` (30/30 Baseline mit existierenden Queries)
- Integration in `cli.py` (search_web + search_batch routing)
- Engine-Config in `dev/search_pipeline/config.yml` und ggf. eigenständige Config-Section
- Routing in `src/search/search_web.py` für parallele Engine-Calls neben Google/Scholar/CrossRef

## Why

User-Profil: science / GitHub / reddit / Dokumentation / hard-info — wenig news. HN-Algolia liefert tech-affine Discussion-Treffer, ergänzt General-Engines um Quellen die User-Profil direkt treffen. Pure HTTP architecture: keine Browser-Last, kein Stealth/CAPTCHA-Risiko, eigenes Rate-Limit unabhängig vom Google-Detection-Limit.

## Where

- `src/search/engines/hn.py` (new, ~150 LOC analog zu `crossref.py`)
- `src/search/search_web.py` (engine routing extension)
- `dev/search_pipeline/03_hn_smoke.py` (new, smoke harness)
- `dev/search_pipeline/config.yml` (HN-config block)
- `cli.py` (search_web + search_batch dispatch)

## API Details

- Endpoint base: `https://hn.algolia.com/api/v1`
- Search: `/search?query=<q>&tags=story&hitsPerPage=20`
- Default filter: `tags=story` (excl. comments)
- Fallback URL bei missing `url`: `https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=<objectID>`
- Kein Auth, kein API-Key
- Rate-Limit: nicht offiziell dokumentiert, empirisch beim ersten Smoke zu messen

## Recherche-Kontext

Siehe `decisions/search05_engine_expansion.md` (commit b55c2e6) für vollständige Engine-Landscape-Analyse: Stract dropped ($27/mo), Marginalia deferred, HN+SE als nächste API-Engines, Domain-Boost-Layer obsolete weil HN+SE die Quellen direkt liefern.

## Out of Scope

- Stack-Exchange-API (separate Bead nach HN-Stabilisierung)
- Marginalia try-or-drop (separate Bead, später)
- Bing-Repair (separat, Selektor-Drift)
- Domain-Boost-Layer in Re-Rank (deferred per search05)
title: searxng: HN-Algolia engine implementation

close_reason: Implemented in commit f7a78ca on dev. Punkt 1 (Scholar polling) implemented as 15→1 cycles (sogar aggressiver als der ursprünglich vorgeschlagene 15→3, basierend auf uniform_timing_probe das 0 cycles in 12 queries zeigte). Punkt 2 (consent sleeps in google.py + scholar.py) entfernt — empirisch 0 events fired in der Probe. Punkt 3 (HTTP rate-limiter 4→30 req/60s) NICHT implemented — durch das Timing Audit (Bead 98s) und die uniform-across-engines Rule wurde entschieden, alle 8 Engines bei 4 req/60s zu lassen für Konsistenz. Die ursprüngliche Argumentation für 30/60s war 'kein bucket-exhaust bei N=3 concurrent', aber bei N=12 funktionierte 4/60s einwandfrei mit erwartbarer Token-Refill-Wartezeit. Asymmetrie HTTP vs Browser bei Rate-Limitern wäre ohne empirische Notwendigkeit gewesen.
description:
## What

Migration dreier Timing-Konstanten von defensiv-konservativ auf aggressiv, basierend auf empirischer Ablation die zero Result-Loss und nominellen Latenz-Gewinn zeigt.

### Punkt 1 — Scholar-Polling-Reduktion

`src/search/engines/scholar.py:17-18` — von `MAX_WAIT_CYCLES=15, WAIT_INTERVAL=1.0` (worst case 15s polling) auf `MAX_WAIT_CYCLES=3, WAIT_INTERVAL=0.2` (worst case 0.6s). Aligned Scholar mit den anderen vier Browser-Engines (Google/DDG/Mojeek/Lobsters) die alle 3×0.2 verwenden. Empirisch lädt Scholar in der Probe in 100% der Fälle bevor der erste Poll feuert — die 14 zusätzlichen Sekunden Defensive sind reine Tail-Latenz wenn die Page mal langsamer rendert.

### Punkt 2 — Consent-Sleep-Eliminierung

`src/search/engines/scholar.py:101` und `src/search/engines/google.py:132` — `await asyncio.sleep(2.0)` nach `_handle_consent` entfernen. Der Sleep war defensive Pause nach Consent-Button-Click. Im Sunny-Path (SOCS-Cookie-Injection vor Navigation) feuert der Consent-Banner nicht, der Sleep also auch nicht. Im seltenen Fallback-Pfad zeigt die Probe keinen messbaren Δ — DOM settled instant nach Click.

### Punkt 3 — HTTP-Engine Rate-Limiter Differenzierung

`src/search/engines/openalex.py:17`, `src/search/engines/crossref.py:18`, `src/search/engines/stack_exchange.py:18` — `_limiters[name] = RateLimiter(max_requests=4, window_seconds=60)` auf `max_requests=30, window_seconds=60` ändern. Browser-Engines (Google/Scholar/DDG/Mojeek/Lobsters) bleiben bei `4/60` wegen Anti-Bot-Vorsicht. Per-Call-Latenz-Effekt im Probe gleich null — die Differenzierung wirkt erst bei sequentiellen Batches (search_batch mit N>4 Queries) wo das Token-Bucket erschöpft.

## Why

Empirische Evidenz aus `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/timing_ablation_20260507_034522.md` (commit fd15083, gemerged auf dev).

Probe ran 3 Queries (python asyncio, tolkien hobbit, sparse retrieval models) zweimal — einmal mit CONFIG A (status quo), einmal mit CONFIG B (aggressiv) — concurrent fan-out über alle 8 Engines pro Query, 2-min Cooldown zwischen Configs.

Befunde:

- Per-engine Latenz-Δ (A vs B mean, 3 Queries gemittelt): alle 8 Engines innerhalb ±53ms außer CrossRef (+283ms — Server-seitige Variance, config-unabhängig) und Mojeek (-207ms — natürliche Variance). Alle innerhalb natürlichem Per-Call-Jitter.

- Result-Set Jaccard A∩B/A∪B: 5 deterministische API-Engines (OpenAlex, CrossRef, Stack Exchange, Mojeek, Lobsters) alle 1.000 (identische Results). 3 SERP-Engines (Google, Scholar, DDG) zwischen 0.267-1.000 — natürliche SERP-Variabilität zwischen Sessions, NICHT durch CONFIG B verursacht (URL-Diff-Inspektion bestätigt).

Bottom line: aggressive Config ist result-equivalent zu Status-Quo. Latenz-Gewinn ist nicht in den Worst-Case-Mittelwerten sichtbar sondern in p99 Tails — Scholars 15s-Polling feuert in der Praxis nie, aber wenn die Scholar-Page mal langsamer lädt, würden 14s die ganze User-Query blockieren. Cut auf 0.6s eliminiert diese Tail-Risk ohne den Median zu beeinflussen.

## Where

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| src/search/engines/scholar.py | MAX_WAIT_CYCLES 15→3, WAIT_INTERVAL 1.0→0.2; asyncio.sleep(2.0) nach _JS_CONSENT entfernen |
| src/search/engines/google.py | asyncio.sleep(2.0) nach _JS_CONSENT entfernen |
| src/search/engines/openalex.py | _limiters max_requests 4→30 |
| src/search/engines/crossref.py | _limiters max_requests 4→30 |
| src/search/engines/stack_exchange.py | _limiters max_requests 4→30 |
| decisions/stealth00_engine_status.md | Update Scholar `wait_for_results` Eintrag (15×1 → 3×0.2) und HTTP-Engine Rate-Limit Differenzierung dokumentieren |

## Verification

- `dev/search_pipeline/13_timing_ablation.py` (auf dev) erneut laufen lassen post-migration — should still show A==B (jetzt mit gemerged Code statt monkeypatch)
- `dev/search_pipeline/11_pipeline_smoke.py` re-run für end-to-end production-shape post-migration
- Für HTTP-Rate-Limiter-Change: `02_burst_smoke.py --queries-per-burst 6` als Lasttest, dass OpenAlex/CrossRef/SE bei 6+ Queries/min ohne 429 durchkommen

## Approach

Klein und fokussiert — 5 src/-Files, primär Konstanten-Änderungen. Plan-Pflicht wegen decisions/-Update sowie weil der dritte Punkt eine Architektur-Entscheidung ist (Rate-Limiter pro Engine-Klasse statt uniform). Worker macht Phase A mit exakten line-references und Decision-Text-Update-Vorschlag, dann Phase B Implementation.

## Bezug zum Probe-Material

Probe-Daten: `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/timing_ablation_20260507_034522.md`
Probe-Script: `dev/search_pipeline/13_timing_ablation.py`
Vor-Probe-Inventur: max-probe Phase A Report 2026-05-07 (siehe Session-History) — vollständige Patchability-Analyse aller drei Konstanten-Klassen.

description:
Session 2026-05-03: während Lobste.rs-Worker das `dev/search_pipeline/05_search_smoke.py --engines google duckduckgo mojeek lobsters` lief, sprang Ghostty's Memory-Display auf "5TB working memory" (kernel virtual memory pressure / pagefile spiral). macOS OOM-killer terminierte den tmux-Server → Lobste.rs-Worker + Preview-Worker tmux-Sessions tot. Lobsters-Worker hatte ZERO Commits → Phase A Arbeit verloren (Worktree existiert aber leer).

## Was wir empirisch wissen

- 4-Engine-Smoke war zum Crash-Zeitpunkt aktiv (Lobsters-Worker startete den Run)
- Mehrere Worker tmux-Sessions waren parallel alive (lobsters + preview, plus ghosts wenn nicht gekillt)
- Jeder alive Worker hat sein eigenes Chrome-Subprocess via `src/search/browser.py`
- Empirisch gemessener Mojeek-Smoke (06): IP-Block bei ~9 queries in 7.5s (1.2 req/s) — bedeutet aktive HTTP-Activity, viele offene Tabs
- Pre-existing Drift: smoke runs sind engine-isolation, gehen NICHT durch search_web-Orchestrator und damit nicht durch shared-Chrome-Singleton

## Hypothesen (alle unverifiziert)

### 1. Tab-cleanup-Leak in `src/search/browser.py`
`tab.close()` im engine `finally`-Block schließt CDP-Tab aber Chrome-Process gibt RAM nicht zwischen Cycles frei. Bei 30 Queries × 4 Engines = 120 Tab-Cycles in 7-8min wäre kumulierter RAM-Druck spürbar.
Verify: Activity Monitor während kleinerem Smoke (Google + DDG, 30 queries) — RAM-Wachstum tracken, vor und nach jedem 10-Query-Block messen.

### 2. Multi-Worker-Chrome-Multiplikation
Jeder Worker hat eigenen Chrome → preview + mojeek + lobsters parallel = 3+ Chrome-Subprocesses. Plus Opus' Main Chrome via search_web. Cumulative RAM × 4-5 Browser-Pools.
Verify: `ps aux | grep Chrome | wc -l` während Multi-Worker-Phase.

### 3. Smoke-Script Resource-Handling
05_search_smoke.py nutzt `start_browser()` + `new_tab()` per Query — möglicherweise keine konsistente Tab-cleanup-Garantie wenn Engine timeout/error.
Verify: Code-Audit von 05_search_smoke.py auf alle Exit-Pfade — wird tab.close() in jedem Fall aufgerufen?

### 4. Pydoll session_dir not isolated between workers
Workers haben evtl. shared `~/.searxng-mcp/browser-session-smoke/` → Lockfile-Konflikte → Chrome zombie-Prozesse die nicht beendet werden.
Verify: `ls ~/.searxng-mcp/browser-session-smoke/` zwischen Worker-Spawns prüfen, Singleton-Lock-Files prüfen.

## Investigation-Plan (Vorschlag)

1. **Reproduce kontrolliert klein:** Single-Worker, 2-Engine-Smoke (Google + DDG), Activity Monitor offen — sehen ob das Pattern auftritt
2. **Skalieren:** dann 3 Engines, dann 4 — RAM-Kurve über Queries plotten
3. **Multi-Worker-Test:** mehrere Worker mit eigener Smoke laufen lassen (nicht 4-engine, nur 1-engine je) — sehen ob das Pattern multi-Chrome-bedingt ist
4. **Tab-cleanup-Audit:** Code-Review der Engine-Files + Smoke-Files auf alle Exit-Pfade

## Out of Scope

- Migration zu single-Browser-singleton-für-alle-Workers (architektonisch größer)
- Resource-cap-Implementation (erst Diagnose, dann Lösung)

## KPI

- Reproduzierbare Memory-Kurve (RAM vs Query-Index) für 1/2/3/4-Engine-Smoke
- Identifizierter Root-Cause (Hypothese verifiziert oder falsifiziert)
- Mitigation-Vorschlag dokumentiert in proj_searxng/browser-pool.md (NEU per Recap-Staging)
title: searxng: Forensic — 5TB memory pressure during 4-engine smoke

description:
Lobste.rs als vierter Engine im Stack — Tech-Specialty-Aggregator. Browser/pydoll Pattern (Konsistenz mit Google/DDG/Mojeek), lobste.rs/search-Endpoint, 4 req/min uniformer Rate-Limit.

## Why

Specialty-Engine nach drei General-Engines. Curated Tech-Community — andere Content-Domain als General-SERP, hohes Signal-zu-Rausch. Kein API, kein Free-Tier-Issue. Liefert zusätzlich eine bewusst minimale Snippet-Variante (nur Domain) als Baseline für Snippet-Vergleich.

## What

- Neue Engine `src/search/engines/lobsters.py` (BaseEngine subclass, mirror mojeek.py)
- Smoke `dev/search_pipeline/07_lobsters_smoke.py` (mirror 06_mojeek_smoke.py)
- Config-Block in `config.yml` (`lobsters:`)
- Routing in `src/search/search_web.py`
- Pre-register `_limiters["lobsters"]` mit max_requests=4, window_seconds=60
- DOCS analog Mojeek-Pattern

## Endpoint

`https://lobste.rs/search?q={query}&what=stories&order=relevance` (GET)

## Selectors (xpath aus SearXNG → CSS)

- Result containers: `li.story`
- URL: `a.u-url` (href)
- Title: `a.u-url` (textContent)
- Snippet: `a.domain` (NUR Domain-Text, keine description — siehe Caveat)

## Snippet Caveat

Lobste.rs ist Link-Aggregator. Listings haben keinen Body-Text auf der Übersichtsseite, nur Title + linked URL + Domain + Tags + Comments-Count + Submitter. Per SearXNG-Pattern: Snippet = nur Domain. By design.

Das ist nützlich für Snippet-Vergleich (deliberate minimal baseline) — og:description aus Preview-Fetch füllt das mit echter Page-Beschreibung.

## Final Smoke Run (Phase B Erweiterung)

Nach Engine + 07_lobsters_smoke fertig: zusätzlich 05_search_smoke laufen lassen mit allen 4 Engines (`--engines google duckduckgo mojeek lobsters`) — Report für Snippet-Vergleichs-Daten.

## Out of Scope

- Pagination
- CAPTCHA-Detection (kein Mechanismus dokumentiert, fall-through wie bei Mojeek)
- Tags/Comments/Submitter im Snippet (würde SearXNG-Pattern verlassen, später optional)

## KPI

- 07_lobsters_smoke.py liefert Status-Breakdown
- 05_search_smoke mit 4 engines liefert Comparison-Report im 01_reports/
- Live CLI test: searxng-cli search_web "<query>" --engines lobsters ≥3 hits (kleinere Index-Größe als General-Engines erwartet)
title: searxng: Lobste.rs engine implementation (browser/pydoll, tech-specialty)

description:
## What

Vollständige Timing-Inventur über alle 8 Engines (5 Browser + 3 HTTP) plus Workflow-Layer-Settings (preview, cache, rate_limiter), mit empirischer Cross-Reference gegen existierende Probe-Reports.

Output: `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/timing_audit_20260507_120000.md` (commit 17f8798).

## Headline-Befund

**Aktuelle uniform-scheme über alle 8 Engines: 4 req/60s rate-limiter, 20s browser-page-load, 15s HTTP-timeout. Alle drei Werte empirisch validiert.**

Nur 5 Deviations existieren in src/. Davon ist eine real (Scholar polling 15×1s vs anderen 3×0.2s), eine harmlos (consent-sleep 2s ist dead code unter SOCS-Cookie-Injection), eine code-konsistenz-unschönheit (Google hat inline-consent-detection, Scholar nicht), zwei sind workflow-strukturell (engine_timeout nicht durchgereicht im default-pfad — quasi-Bug; rate_limiter.py module-default dead code).

## Validated (kann so bleiben)

- Alle 8 rate-limiter bei 4 req/60s (timing_ablation N≤3)
- Alle 5 browser page-load timeouts bei 20s (max_results_probe: 4-23x headroom)
- HTTP timeouts bei 15s für OpenAlex (6x headroom) und CrossRef (4x headroom)
- Google/Scholar consent-sleep 2.0s — empirisch dead code unter SOCS

## UNVALIDATED (Probe nötig wenn wir clean lock-in wollen)

1. Scholar polling 15×1s vs 3×0.2s — Probe zeigt 3 cycles reicht
2. Stack Exchange timeout 15s — 30x over-provisioned (max observed 489ms)
3. HTTP rate-limiter 4/60 für N>3 concurrent (batch workflows untested)
4. Preview timeout 3s + concurrency 8 — keine Latenz-Verteilung gemessen
5. BACKOFF_BASE 30s — Formel nie unter echtem 429 getestet
6. `engine_timeout=None` im default _query_engines_concurrent path — structural gap, kein watchdog wenn caller das nicht setzt
7. Cache TTL 3600s — keine hit-rate-Daten

## Surprises

- `rate_limiter.py` MAX_REQUESTS=10 Default ist dead code (alle 8 engines injizieren eigene RateLimiter zur import-time). Neue Engine ohne diesen Pattern würde silent zu 10 req/60s defaulten. Documentation/Enforcement nötig.
- engine_timeout wired asymmetrisch — exists auf der Workflow-Signatur aber `_query_engines_concurrent` (default-Pfad) ruft `_engine_with_timing` ohne timeout-Argument auf.

## Next Steps (wenn user lock-in will)

Aus dem Audit-Report Section 5 zwei Listen ableiten: Werte die "as is" bleiben (validiert) und Werte die einen Probe-Run brauchen. Beadkonkret:

- searxng-8dg (Scholar polling + HTTP rate + consent sleeps) — der Audit bestätigt dass die ersten zwei Punkte aggressiv sicher sind, der dritte (consent) dead code; closeable wenn user Migration approved
- Optional neuer probe-Bead für Punkt 2 (SE timeout sweep) und Punkt 3 (rate-limiter at N>3)
- Bug-fix: engine_timeout im default-pfad durchreichen (separater kleiner Bead)

## Bezüge

- Probe-Report: `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/timing_audit_20260507_120000.md`
- Probes referenziert: `timing_ablation_20260507_034522.md`, `max_results_probe_20260507_024429.md`
- Existierender Bead: searxng-8dg (Timing-Migration) wird durch dieses Audit informiert
title: Timing Audit — per-engine inventory + empirical justification

description:
Pool steht: 8 aktive Engines (Google, DDG, Mojeek, Lobsters, Scholar, CrossRef, OpenAlex, Stack Exchange) im uniform 4 req/min Rate-Limit. Full-pool 8-engine Smoke-Daten verfügbar in `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/search_smoke_20260504_023641.md` (30 Queries, alle 8 Engines, overlap 1-7 pro Query, max 7-Engine-Match auf q10 RLHF).

## Why

User-Beobachtung deferred bis nach Pool-Stand: aktuelle `_deduplicate()` in `src/search/search_web.py` ist nur first-occurrence-wins URL-Dedup, KEIN Overlap-basiertes Ranking. Plus: Snippet-Bloat-Problem (Google bloated, DDG clean, Lobsters domain-only) und structured-Metadata-Lücke für SemSch/Stack Exchange/OpenAlex/CrossRef-Returns die nicht in den flat `snippet`-String passen.

User-Konzept ("absteigend sortiert nach Anzahl Engines die dieselbe URL geliefert haben") existiert NICHT im Code — nur im 05_search_smoke Report-Format als Metrik. Plus: Specialty-Engines wie OpenAlex/Stack Exchange werden bei Overlap-Ranking IMMER nach unten sinken weil ihre URL-Räume nicht mit Google/DDG/Mojeek überlappen.

## Two coupled architecture decisions

### 1. Ranking — Overlap-basiert mit Engine-Klassen-Slots

Aktuelles `_deduplicate(results)` durch overlap-aware-Ranking ersetzen: pro URL die Anzahl Engines zählen die sie geliefert haben, absteigend sortieren. ABER: Specialty-Slots reservieren (e.g. 14 general/tech overlap-ranked + 4 academic + 2 Q&A) damit OpenAlex/SemSch/Stack-Exchange-Treffer nicht durchs Raster fallen. ALTERNATIV: Per-Engine-Position als Boost addieren (Position 1 von Specialty = synthetischer 2-Engine-Overlap-Score). ALTERNATIV: URL-Kanonisierung (arxiv/DOI/SemSch-paper-URL eines Papers vereinheitlichen → echter Overlap zwischen academic engines).

### 2. SearchResult-Format — strukturierte Metadaten

Aktuell flat: `SearchResult(url, title, snippet, engine, position, preview)`. Specialty-Engines liefern: SemSch/OpenAlex (authors, year, citationCount, tldr, externalIds), Stack Exchange (score, answer_count, tags). Heute werden die in `snippet` gequetscht oder nicht abgebildet.

Option A: flat bleiben, snippet als display-string, preview-fetch per-engine optional → 30-line patch
Option B: SearchResult mit `metadata: dict | None`, Renderer in `_format_results` metadata-aware, preview.py skippt Engines mit native abstract → architektur-refactor

## Coupling

Beide Punkte hängen am `_format_results` und am Output-Shape. Sinnvoll EINE Bead, gemeinsam zu entscheiden. Ranking-Implementation OHNE Format-Klärung produziert die "Specialty sinkt durch"-Falle. Format-Implementation OHNE Ranking ist sinnlos weil das Display von Sortierung abhängt.

## Reference

- Aktueller Code: `src/search/search_web.py` (`_deduplicate`, `_format_results`)
- Pool-Smoke-Daten: `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/search_smoke_20260504_023641.md`
- Decisions/search03_ranking.md (STALE, beschreibt pre-engine-cut SearXNG-internal-scoring) — sollte nach Beads-Closure aktualisiert werden

## KPI

- Decision-File `decisions/search07_ranking_format.md` (oder ähnlich) mit Soll-Stand
- Implementation in src/search/search_web.py (overlap counting + engine-classes-slots + metadata field)
- Live Verifikation: search_web_workflow Output-Format passt zu Soll, Specialty-Hits erscheinen in Top 20

## Out of Scope

- Bing-direct Re-Eval (Bead-search05 Drop-Reason Stand)
- Stack Exchange API key registration (Anonym 300/day reicht für jetzt)
- arXiv direct fanout (eigenes Bead falls relevant)
title: searxng: Ranking + SearchResult-Format Architektur

description:
## What

Persistent inventory of project improvement levers organized by pipeline layer. Identified during 2026-05-07 session as systematic alternative to ad-hoc lever-finding.

Six layer classes, each with 3-5 sub-levers in different maturity states (quick-win, substantial, speculative).

## Layers and Sub-Levers

### Engine Layer

- **Per-engine query reformulation** — currently we send identical query to all 8 engines. Each engine has its own syntax (Google: `site:`, exact-phrase quoting; OpenAlex: `filter=type:article`; CrossRef: `filter=from-pub-date`). Per-engine adapter would translate one logical query into engine-native syntax.
- **Per-engine quality signals into ranking fusion** — OpenAlex provides cited_by_count, CrossRef provides is-referenced-by-count, Google provides only rank-position. Currently not factored into our merge-and-rank.
- **Engine health monitoring** — currently we discover engine drift through user reports (Bing DOM-drift, HN rate-cascade). Self-metrics: empty-result-rate, parsing-error-rate, response-time per engine. Would surface drift early.
- **OpenAlex two-step search for disambiguation** — autocomplete concept-id, then `filter=concepts.id:C...&search=X`. Hypothesis on the architecture/Bauhistorik observation; needs evidence first that off-topic results are recurring.
- **CrossRef polite-pool mailto** — implemented this session. (Closed by Task 2 in cf-md-probe worker.)
- **Stack Exchange sort=relevance + multi-site** — relevance sort implemented this session. Multi-site (math.SE, cs.SE for academic queries) still open.

### Ranking / Aggregation Layer

- **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)** — SOTA for heterogeneous multi-engine fusion. We currently use hard slot-allocation 12/6/2. RRF would combine engine ranks weighted by reciprocal of position.
- **Semantic deduplication** — currently dedup is URL-only. Academic papers often appear as DOI + arxiv preprint + publisher URL = three URLs for one paper. Title/abstract similarity would dedupe semantically.
- **Score normalization across engines** — Mojeek's score and Google's rank-position are not directly comparable. If we mix into a single ranked list, normalization needed.
- **Query-aware allocation** — fixed 12/6/2 ignores that 'how to implement X in Python' is QA-heavy while 'machine learning theory' is academic-heavy. Dynamic allocation based on query classification.

### Snippet Layer

- **Per-engine structured snippets** — Mojeek delivers bolding, Google delivers term-highlighting. Are we capturing these in our snippet selection? If not, we lose engine-provided emphasis signals.
- **Query-match highlighting** — mark query terms in snippet for user. UI-side improvement.
- **Multi-source snippet fusion** — currently best-of (og:description vs meta:description vs search-snippet) by clean-len × lexical-density. Could fuse complementary content from multiple sources instead of best-of.

### Caching Layer

- **Per-query-class TTL** — news-queries short, reference-queries long. Currently flat 1h.
- **Negative cache for 0-result queries** — avoid re-querying engines that returned nothing for a query.
- **Speculative pre-crawl** — after search returns top-N URLs, pre-crawl them so subsequent scrape_url is cache-hit.

### Scraper Layer

- **Cloudflare/Vercel markdown fast-path** — IMPLEMENTED this session in cf-md-probe worker. (Closed when worker merges Task 1.)
- **Conditional GET (If-Modified-Since)** — re-scrape only changed pages. Requires per-URL Last-Modified tracking.
- **Multi-page aggregation** — when a tutorial is paginated (5 parts), fetch all and concatenate. Heuristic: 'Next page' link detection.

### Self-Improvement / Observability Layer

- **Slot-underflow tracking** — how often do we fail to fill the 12/6/2 slots? If frequent, allocation is mis-dimensioned.
- **Snippet floor-fallback tracking** — how often do we hit MIN_FLOOR=40 best-of-worst fallback? High rate = snippet-source-chain weak.
- **Engine-drift detection alerts** — engine X normally returns 15-20 results, returns 0-3 for N consecutive sessions → alert.
- **A/B testing harness** — when comparing strategy A vs B, need infrastructure to run both side-by-side on same query corpus.

## Approach

This bead is a backlog tracker, not a single deliverable. Each implemented sub-lever:
- Closes its own sub-task or a separate bead linked here via comment
- Updates this bead's description (or appends comment) to mark IMPLEMENTED with date + commit reference

Closing condition: this bead closes when all sub-levers are either implemented, explicitly rejected with reason, or moved to dedicated beads. Until then it stays open as the persistent prioritization base for future sessions.

## Related Source

Layer-class identification originated in 2026-05-07 session after substance-audit pushback — see session chat history for full reasoning behind each lever choice. Cloudflare-fast-path lever (scraper layer) was the trigger that surfaced the systematic gap.
title: Layer-based improvement inventory — 6 pipeline layers, sub-levers as backlog

description:
## Why

Bead dl9 hat den finalen pipeline_smoke v5 baseline mit 600 URLs und einem snippet_quality_20260505_223506.md analysis report produziert. Der Analysis-Report rankt Sources nach length×density (Usefulness-Score) und zeigt empirisch welche Source pro URL das längste/dichteste Snippet liefert.

Was die length×density Heuristik NICHT misst: ob der gewählte Snippet inhaltlich zur URL passt. Ein langer, sauberer, dichter Text der über das falsche Thema spricht würde nach unserer Heuristik "gewinnen", wäre aber für den User nutzlos. Das ist die User-Frage "macht der Snippet Sinn für die URL".

## Approach

Diese Untersuchung scraped die URLs aus der Singular-Baseline und vergleicht den gewählten Snippet (snippet_source field aus pipeline_smoke) gegen den tatsächlichen Page-Inhalt. Output: Side-by-Side Bewertung pro URL.

## Scope

- Input: dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/pipeline_smoke_20260505_223430.md (oder dessen Nachfolger wenn neuere Baselines existieren — auto-newest pattern wie snippet_quality_analysis.py)
- Stratified Sample: 60 URLs (5 pro Query × 30 Queries × 4 Klassen GENERAL/ACADEMIC/QA + Edge Cases) ODER alle 600 wenn Performance erlaubt
- Scrape via dev/scrape_pipeline (Mode 1 raw oder Mode 2 filtered, beide vorhanden)
- Output: neues investigation report (z.B. dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/snippet_content_fit_<ts>.md) mit pro URL: source | snippet | scraped excerpt | fit_score (oder manual eyeball annotation column)
- Decision-Output: ggf. Anpassung der Priority-Chain in decisions/search07_ranking_format.md basierend auf Findings

## Out of scope (deferred)

- Vollautomatischer fit-Score per Embedding-Similarity (nice-to-have, aber Manual Eyeball reicht erstmal)
- Production-CLI Änderungen — die kommen erst wenn die Empirie eine Priority-Chain-Änderung rechtfertigt

## Dependencies

- Singular-Baseline pipeline_smoke (closed via dl9, Datei existiert auf main)
- Scrape-Pipeline (Mode 1 + Mode 2 existieren auf main, siehe CLAUDE.md scrape components Tabelle)

## Verification

- Investigation Report mit den 60 (oder 600) URL-Snippet-vs-Content Vergleichen
- User-Bewertung: Welche Source-Kategorien liefern systematisch passende Snippets? Welche systematisch unpassende?
- Falls Findings Priority-Chain-Anpassung nahelegen: decisions/search07_ranking_format.md Update + Code-Anpassung in search_web._format_results

## Approach für späteren Worker

1. Phase A: Sample-Strategie definieren (60 vs 600), Scrape-Mode wählen, Vergleichs-Format vorschlagen
2. Phase B: Script schreiben das pro Sample-URL die scraped content fetcht (mit cache, da scrape-pipeline 1h disk cache hat) und ein side-by-side MD generiert
3. User reviewt, gibt Findings
4. Falls Findings Code-Änderung rechtfertigen: separater Worker für Priority-Chain-Adjustment

## Related

- searxng-dl9 (closed) — produced the singular baseline this investigation runs against
- searxng-5gc (closed) — encoding-hygiene fixes that ensured the snippet_source values are clean text (no entities, no mojibake)
- Future: this bead's findings might inform a future Priority-Chain-Adjustment bead
title: Snippet-Content-Fit Verification via Scraping

- Routing in src/search/search_web.py (Import + ENGINES dict entry)

## Why

Q&A Coverage über das ganze Stack-Exchange-Netzwerk. Pure HTTP, keine Browser-Last, eigenständiges Rate-Limit. User-Profil "harte technische Infos" — SE-Netzwerk ist DAS Q&A-Repository.

description:
## What

Persistent question-list per layer-improvement lever (referenced from `searxng-b1j` Layer-Inventory). Each question is a concrete starting point for next-session research — not a hypothesis, an actual ask. Source-tagged so we know whether to use RAG (engine-docs in `searxng_reference`), paper-search via `searxng-cli search_batch --academic`, or live experiment / code reading.

The b1j bead lists WHAT could change. This bead lists WHAT QUESTIONS we ask to design that change.

## Engine Layer

### Per-engine query reformulation
- For each of OpenAlex / CrossRef / Stack Exchange / Mojeek / Google / DDG / Scholar / Lobsters: what query syntax does each accept, what filter/operator surface does each expose? [RAG: searxng_reference, primary]
- Is there a published query-class taxonomy (factoid / navigational / academic / how-to / comparison) usable for routing? [Papers]
- What's the cost/benefit of pre-classifying a query (extra latency for one classifier call) vs. post-filtering merged results? [Live experiment + code reading]
- Are there papers on adaptive query reformulation specifically in metasearch (multi-engine) settings? [Papers — paper-search-batch from 2026-05-07 yielded "Generative query reformulation for effective adhoc search" arxiv 2308.00415, plus Kassaie 2025 on math-search RRF; both worth full reading]

### OpenAlex two-step disambiguation
- How does `/concepts` (deprecated) differ from `/topics`/`/domains`/`/fields`/`/subfields`? [RAG: searxng_reference, OpenAlex docs indexed]
- Does OpenAlex provide a topic-classifier endpoint (free text → concept-IDs)? [RAG] — quick-reference at `developers.openalex.org/guides/llm-quick-reference` is in the index
- What's the latency of an autocomplete-then-filter call vs. a single search call? [Live experiment]
- Is the disambiguation problem common enough in our query corpus to justify the latency cost? [Live experiment — needs query-corpus replay against current pipeline]

### Per-engine quality signals into ranking fusion
- Which engines expose a numeric score we can consume? [RAG: searxng_reference for OpenAlex/CrossRef/Mojeek/SE; code reading for Google/DDG/Lobsters serializers]
- Are scores comparable across engines or only within-engine? [RAG + paper on score normalization]
- For engines without scores, can rank-position serve as a usable proxy? [Papers — RRF research relevant]

### Engine health monitoring
- What metrics best signal DOM-drift before it cascades into user-visible breakage? (parsing-error-rate-per-day timeline, average-result-count-per-engine, response-time variance?) [Paper: "Drifter" arxiv 2309.08617]
- Detection latency target — how fast can we detect a Bing-style DOM drift before users notice? [Live experiment, depends on query volume]
- What's the right log/observability layer to capture per-call empty-result-rates without changing engine code? [Code reading + design]

## Ranking Layer

### Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)
- What's the empirical k in RRF? Original paper (Cormack et al. 2009) used k=60 — is that robust across query classes or learnable? [Papers — Kassaie 2025 explores math-search applications]
- How does RRF compare to weighted CombSUM for our heterogeneous engine set? [Papers — Ryerson "Implementation of meta search engine with different rank aggregation algorithms" tests 4 algorithms]
- Are there hybrid RRF + score-normalization approaches in current literature? [Papers]
- Could RRF replace our slot-allocation entirely, or layer over it? [Code reading — `src/search/search_web.py` `_merge_and_rank`]

### Semantic deduplication
- What's SOTA for cross-source paper dedup (DOI ↔ arxiv preprint ↔ publisher URL)? [Papers — "Survey of Text Deduplication" IEEE 2025 has the field overview]
- Does title-fuzzy-match + author-overlap suffice as cheap baseline, or do we need embedding-based dedup? [Papers + live test against our preview/snippet data]
- How do tools like Connected Papers / Inciteful handle this in production? [Web research]
- Is OpenAlex's external-ID system (DOI/ORCID/ROR/PMID lookup) usable to auto-dedupe at ingest? [RAG: searxng_reference, OpenAlex external-IDs guide]

### Score normalization across engines
- Min-max, z-score, rank-based — which fits our heterogeneous score scales? [Papers]
- Do production metasearch systems publish their normalization strategy? [Web research, GitHub for Searx/Marginalia code]

### Query-aware allocation
- What query-type classifiers are available off-the-shelf (sentence-transformers fine-tuned, T5, simple keyword heuristics)? [Web research + Hugging Face]
- What's the marginal benefit over fixed 12/6/2 in offline eval against our query corpus? [Live experiment]

## Snippet Layer

### Per-engine structured snippets
- Which of our 8 engines return marked-up snippets (Mojeek bold, Google em, etc.)? [RAG: searxng_reference for Mojeek; code reading for others' parsers]
- Worth preserving the markup through display, or strip and re-highlight ourselves? [Design]

### Multi-source snippet fusion
- Beyond best-of-(clean_len × density), are there published methods for combining snippet candidates? [Papers]
- Does query-anchored snippet scoring (snippet-contains-query-terms-weighted) outperform our current scorer? [Live experiment against current snippet selection logs]

### Query-match highlighting
- UI-side feature; mostly design choice not research question. Read what other metasearch UIs do. [Web research]

## Caching Layer

### Per-query-class TTL
- Does query freshness-need correlate with measurable query features (recency-words, news-keywords)? [Papers — IR literature on query-temporal-classification]
- What TTL granularity is empirically supported? [Papers + live]

### Negative cache for 0-result queries
- Cost/benefit of avoiding re-query of empty queries — needs real query distribution data. [Live: log-replay against current cache layer]

### Speculative pre-crawl of search-result top-N
- For top-N URLs from search, does pre-crawl latency offset cache-hit savings on subsequent scrape_url? [Live experiment]
- Are there pre-fetch-with-bandwidth-budget patterns from CDN literature applicable here? [Papers]

## Scraper Layer

### Conditional GET (If-Modified-Since / ETag)
- For URLs we've scraped before, does Last-Modified / ETag tracking save real bytes? [Live: replay against scrape log]
- What proportion of typical scrape-targets support these headers? [Live experiment + measurement]

### Multi-page aggregation
- Heuristic for "Next page" / "Continue reading" detection across docs platforms? [Web research]
- Cost/benefit on tutorial-shaped content vs. risk of over-aggregating? [Live experiment]

## Self-Improvement / Observability Layer

### Slot-underflow tracking
- How often do we fail to fill 12/6/2? Add metric, observe over typical query corpus. [Code change + live observation]

### Snippet floor-fallback tracking
- How often do we hit MIN_FLOOR=40 best-of-worst fallback? Same: add metric, observe. [Code change]

### Engine-drift detection alerts
- Mostly observation-side; see Engine Layer "Engine health monitoring" question above. [Paper: Drifter arxiv 2309.08617 + design]

### A/B testing harness
- What's the minimal viable A/B framework that doesn't require service-level changes? [Design + paper search]

## Approach for next session

Pick one priority lever, start with its tagged source:
- RAG-tagged: open `searxng_reference` collection, run `rag-cli search_hybrid` queries on the precise sub-question, drill via `read_document`
- Papers-tagged: `searxng-cli search_batch --academic <queries>`, evaluate, scrape/index relevant papers
- Live-tagged: code reading + experimental script in `dev/`

Build a dedicated implementation bead with Phase-A design before any code change. This bead remains open as the question backlog index.
title: Research questions per priority lever — next-session research backlog

description:
## Scope

Wraps up session 2026-05-04 work after the entity-fix bead and the skill-rewrite bead are merged:

1. **Full 30-query smoke run** with the new search_web 20-only logic:

   ```
   ./venv/bin/python3 dev/search_pipeline/05_search_smoke.py --engines google duckduckgo mojeek lobsters "google scholar" crossref openalex stack_exchange --max-queries 30
   ```

   Output: new `search_smoke_<ts>.md` in `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/`. Expected: 30/30 queries with results, ~20 URLs per query, slot allocation visible (12 general / 4 academic / 2 Q&A / 2 overflow), no HTML-entity-encoded snippets (after the entity-fix bead is merged). Note: `05_search_smoke.py` may need adjustment since the new SearchResult shape carries `engines: list[str]` and `snippets: dict[str, str]` — verify it still parses correctly into the multi-engine merge it does internally for reporting; if not, minimal-fix the smoke (do not refactor).

2. **Manual relevance review** of the 20-URL output per query — user reads through the new smoke report and judges: are the 20 URLs actually relevant or is the rest noise? description:
Mojeek als dritter unabhängiger General-Search-Engine im Stack. Browser/pydoll Pattern (Konsistenz mit Google + DDG), mojeek.com search-Endpoint, 4 req/min uniformer Rate-Limit.

## Why

Mojeek hat einen eigenen Crawler-Index (NICHT Bing-derivative wie DDG). Damit nach Google + DDG der erste echt unabhängige dritte Index. mvz-Strategie "in die Breite" — Coverage-Diversifikation durch Engine-Diversität. Free, non-commercial, kein API-Key, kein Free-Tier-Limit — anders als die gedroppten 7ue/jmo/civ.

## What

- Neue Engine `src/search/engines/mojeek.py` (BaseEngine subclass, mirror duckduckgo.py)
- Smoke-Script `dev/search_pipeline/06_mojeek_smoke.py` (analog 04_ddg_smoke.py)
- Config-Block in `config.yml` (`mojeek:` neben `duckduckgo:`)
- Routing in `src/search/search_web.py`
- Pre-register Rate-Limiter `_limiters["mojeek"]` mit max_requests=4, window_seconds=60
- DOCS updates analog DDG-Pattern

## Endpoint

`https://www.mojeek.com/search?q={query}&safe=1`

via pydoll Browser-Tab. GET, server-rendered HTML, kein JS für Result-Loading nötig.

## Selectors (aus SearXNG xpath, nach CSS übersetzt)

- Result containers: `ul.results-standard > li > a.ob`
- URL: a-tag href direkt (NO redirect wrapper, anders als DDG's uddg)
- Title: `li > h2 > a`
- Snippet: `li p.s`
- CAPTCHA-Detection: TBD via Phase A DOM probe

## Reuse Worker

Preview-Worker (alive idle ~52% Context) hat den Architektur-Kontext geladen — mojeek folgt exakt dem DDG-Pattern. Kein neuer Spawn.

## Out of Scope

- Pagination (s-Param-Handling — only Page 1)
- Region/Language selection (Default ohne arc/lb)
- Image/News-Search (nur web/general)

## KPI

- 06_mojeek_smoke.py liefert 30/30 OK (oder dokumentierter non-OK Breakdown)
- Live CLI test: `searxng-cli search_web "<query>" --engines mojeek` ≥5 Hits
- Smoke-Report im 01_reports/ Format
title: searxng: Mojeek engine implementation (browser/pydoll, third independent index)

description:
## Problem

After Bead a45 implementation (commit 4940318, merged 5b48043), the new search_web 20-only output renders some snippets with HTML-encoded entities instead of decoded characters. Q4 demo (Mietvertrag query, Result #13 https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-662-56074-7_84 in `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/search_web_demo_20260504_202311.md`) shows:

> Snippet: Blank, Der Wegfall des Eigenbedarfs nach Ablauf der Kündigungsfrist, NJW 2006...

Should render: `Kündigungsfrist`, `NJW` followed by non-breaking space `2006`.

## Root cause (TWO sources, both surface entity-encoded text)

1. `src/search/engines/openalex.py._reconstruct_abstract` — joins inverted-index tokens as-is. OpenAlex stores tokens with HTML entities pre-encoded for some German academic works (Springer-published in particular).

2. `src/search/preview.py._fetch_one` — Springer-style og:description meta tags contain HTML-encoded entities. When `_select_snippet` falls through to the og preview rule, the entities pass through.

Worker bead-a45-impl traced both during debug. Worker tried a Rule-7 fix in `search_web.py._select_snippet` (Google/Scholar fallback path), which is the WRONG location for these two sources — that fix was reverted-by-not-touching but still committed.

## Fix

Add `html.unescape()` at TWO sites:

- **`src/search/engines/openalex.py._reconstruct_abstract`** — wrap the joined abstract in `html.unescape(...)` before return. Add `import html` at top if not present.
- **`src/search/preview.py._fetch_one`** — wrap the extracted `og` and `meta` strings in `html.unescape(...)` before returning the dict. Add `import html` if not present.

Two-line behavioral change plus 1-2 import lines.

## Verification

Re-run Q4 demo:

```
./venv/bin/python3 cli.py search_web "Mietvertrag Kündigungsfrist gesetzliche Regelung" --language de
```

Result #13 snippet must show `Kündigungsfrist` not `Kündigungsfrist`. Then full 30-query smoke (separate bead).

## Out of scope

- Other engines' entity handling (Google/Scholar/CrossRef already covered or not affected)
- Any rewrite of the snippet selection logic — this is a leaf fix in two engines
title: OpenAlex + og-preview HTML entity decoding fix in snippet selection

description:
Session 2026-04-17 discussed adding a URL content preview to search results. Two interpretations emerged:

## Interpretation 1 (NOT this bead — in searxng-mvz)
Fix the existing engine-snippet extraction. The snippet field from Google SERP HTML is already captured via CSS selectors (.VwiC3b, [data-sncf], .lEBKkf, [data-content-feature]) but may be stale due to Google DOM changes. This is the 'selector drift' check already in searxng-mvz OPEN.

## Interpretation 2 (THIS bead)
Add a REAL URL content preview by fetching each result URL and extracting a richer preview than the search engine's SERP snippet.

## What

Per search result, fetch the target URL and extract:
- <title> tag
- Open Graph description (og:description meta tag)
- First ~200 chars of cleaned article text (via readability / trafilatura)
Show all three (or best available) as a 'preview' block under the search result, distinct from the engine snippet.

## Why

SearXNG's preview = engine snippet only (verified via GitHub research on searxng/searxng 2026-04-17: grep for 'preview' returned 0 matches in *.py; engines just extract from SERP HTML). Google snippets are often truncated or auto-generated from misleading SERP fragments. Fetching the actual page gives the user a better signal before clicking.

## Where

- Integration point: src/search/search_web.py after deduplication, before formatting
- Fetcher: reuse src/scraper/scrape_url.py (filtered content) or scrape_url_raw.py (title + og only, faster)
- Response shape: extend SearchResult dataclass in src/search/result.py with 'preview' field (optional, null if fetch failed or was skipped)

## Cost / Performance Considerations

- 10 results × scrape_url = 10 additional page loads per search = ~5-15s latency
- Options to mitigate:
  (a) async parallel fetch of top-N URLs (e.g. top 5 only)
  (b) timeout aggressively (3s per URL, fallback to null preview)
  (c) opt-in flag (preview=true param on search_web) — default off
  (d) cache URL preview keyed by URL for 24h

## Dependencies

- searxng-mvz must be resolved — engine reliability is prerequisite (no point previewing 0 results)
- searxng-cli migration might change the API surface; coordinate with that bead

## Related Beads

- searxng-mvz (engine reliability prerequisite)
- searxng-31z (scrape_url garbage detection — preview calls scrape_url internally, must handle garbage responses gracefully)

## Research Already Done

GitHub search on searxng/searxng confirmed: no preview plugin exists in SearXNG. SearXNG's 'preview' is just the engine snippet extracted from SERP HTML via xpath (for Google: div.ilUpNd.H66NU.aSRlid). Our implementation already does the equivalent via CSS selectors. A real URL-fetch preview would be a NEW feature, not a port.
title: searxng: URL content preview feature (beyond engine snippet)

description:
## What

Foundation-Implementation BEVOR Stage 2 (--pdf/--docs/--books-Flags in Bead x4f). Drei Code-Änderungen die unabhängig vom Filter-Design Wert haben und die Pool-Tiefe für die Filter-Flags vorbereiten.

### Punkt 1 — Google-Selector-Fix

`src/search/engines/google.py` — `_JS_PARSE` und `_JS_WAIT` von `#rso h3` auf `div.MjjYud` umstellen. Pro Container Titel flexibel finden: `h3` → `.LC20lb` → erstes `a[href^="http"]`. Erwarteter Effekt: Google liefert ~19 statt ~9 Treffer pro Single-Call.

Empirisch belegt durch `dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/google_selector_probe_20260507_025225.md` — Probe zählte 19 `div.MjjYud`-Container im DOM bei `num=100`, aber nur 9 davon haben h3-Titel; die anderen 10 nutzen `.LC20lb`/h2/span.

### Punkt 2 — max_results-Bump pro Engine-Typ

`src/search/search_web.py` Zeilen 63 und 72 — die hardcoded `10` durch eine Konstante ersetzen, dann pro Engine-Typ unterschiedliche Werte:

- Browser-Engines (Google, Scholar, DDG, Mojeek, Lobsters): max_results=100 (Google nutzt `num=100` URL-Parameter; DDG/Mojeek slicen aus DOM was die Page liefert — bei 10 strukturell gedeckelt; Scholar cappt bei 20 intern; Lobsters je nach Query)
- HTTP-Engines OpenAlex und CrossRef: max_results=200 (deren Doku-Limits, beide trivial skalierbar)
- Stack Exchange: max_results=100 (hard cap der SE-API)

Mechanisch: Konstante in search_web.py extrahieren plus per-Engine-Override-Map oder Engine-eigene-Methode `engine.default_max_results()`. Designentscheidung im Phase-A-Plan.

### Punkt 3 — Free-Word-Injection-Mechanik

In `cli.py` (oder im Workflow vor dem Engine-Fan-out) — Mechanik um an die Query ein freies Wort anzuhängen abhängig von einer Flag. Konkret: wenn Stage-2-Flag `--pdf` aktiv → Query wird zu `"<query> pdf"`; wenn `--books` → `"<query> book"`; wenn `--docs` → `"<query> documentation"` (Wort tunable pro Flag).

In Stage 1 wird nur die Mechanik gebaut (Default-off), nicht die Flags selbst — die kommen in Stage 2 (Bead x4f). Aber die Mechanik muss da sein damit Stage 2 sie nutzen kann.

## Why

Empirische Evidenz aus dem 2026-05-07-Probe-Trio (siehe Bead x4f-Kommentar):

- max_results probe (commit f097395): Pool-Tiefe heute ~80 URLs/Query, mit max_results-Bump erreichbar ~570
- Google-Selector probe (commit 6b66b31): Selector-Bug isoliert, +10 Google-Treffer pro Query möglich
- Free-Word probe (in Flight): testet die Density-Steigerung durch Wort-Anhängen — Ergebnis pending, wird auf x4f als Folge-Kommentar dokumentiert

Pool-Tiefe ist Vorbedingung für nutzbare Filter — ohne diese Foundation würden die Stage-2-Flags bei eng gefilterten Queries (--pdf/--books bei untypischen Queries) leer ausgehen.

## Where

| File | Change |
|------|--------|

Bing ist Microsoft's eigener Index, distinkt von Google + Mojeek. DDG zieht aus Bing aber mit eigenen Snippets — wir kriegen mit Bing direct: Bing's eigene Snippet-Generation, optional News/Image-Sub-Pipelines, andere Ranking-Logik. Coverage-Plus für "in die Breite". Plus: wenn das Repair gelingt, ist das ein Test ob unsere Selector-Stabilität langfristig hält oder ob wir periodisch ALLE Engine-Selectors verifizieren müssen.

## Scope

description:
Session 2026-04-16 attempted to build a tier-ranking methodology across 4 engines (google, bing, google scholar, crossref) but uncovered blockers in the search pipeline that must be resolved first. Scope for next session: JUST get Google producing 30/30 reliable results via headless pydoll. Everything else (Bing/Scholar parsers, tier-ranking eval, prod migration) waits until Google is rock-solid.

## What

Build a minimal, reproducible Google-only smoke test that produces 30/30 OK results for the 30 baseline queries in headless pydoll Chrome. Once stable: re-enable bing / scholar / crossref one by one, then run the tier-ranking eval (tooling already exists as WIP).

## Why

All downstream search work (multi-engine eval, tier ranking methodology, prod migration from MCP to CLI) depends on having AT LEAST ONE browser-based engine that reliably produces results. Current state: only crossref (httpx, no browser) works consistently. Google shows inline consent banner + rate-limits after 1-2 queries.

## Where

- **Scaffold (ready, untested):** dev/search_pipeline/new/
  - config.yml — single source of truth (browser, stealth, google, run, detection, report)
  - queries.txt — 30 baseline queries (Tech/Code 8 + Science 6 + German 6 + Niche 5 + Broad 5)
  - 01_google_smoke.py — headless pydoll, shared browser, per-query detection signals
  - 01_reports/ — empty, smoke never completed
- **WIP Tier-Ranking (ready, blocked on data):** dev/search_pipeline/weights_eval/
  - 14_tiered_ranking.py, 15_tier_ranking_eval.py, 11_engine_isolation.py (Event-Loop-Fix), _isolation_report.py
- **Prod pipeline:** src/search/ (browser.py, engines/google.py) — next session's fixes must transfer here eventually

## Key Session Findings

1. **Event-Loop-Per-Call Bug (H4) FIXED** — commit 3da6411. fetch_search_results did asyncio.run() per call, killed the browser singleton's CDP websocket after first call. Fix: single asyncio.run() batches all queries in 11_engine_isolation.py. Applies to prod eventually (MCP→CLI move makes prod pattern = dev pattern).

2. **Google inline consent banner (DISCOVERY, UNVERIFIED)** — Google now shows 'Before you continue / We use cookies and data' INLINE on the search URL, no redirect to consent.google.com. Current prod code only handles the redirect case. Worker added _has_inline_consent() detection to 01_google_smoke.py (body-text check), restart was interrupted — fix works in theory, not verified end-to-end.

3. **Detection signals defined** — per query: url_count, unique_domains, page_title, current_url, google_internal_urls, status_label (OK / BLOCKED / CAPTCHA / CONSENT / EMPTY / SUSPECT). Status rules codified in config.yml.

4. **Stresstest ≠ Prod** — 2026-04-07 30/30 baseline was dev/engines_eval/28_stress_test.py with its own _stress_browser.py config, NOT src/search/browser.py. Prod pipeline was never actually tested at 30-query volume.

5. **Docker/Tor cleanup done** — commits 6bfdc29 + 3796da1. searxng-docker container, tor container, docker-compose.yml, top-level searxng/settings.yml all gone. Prod is pure pydoll.

## Next Session Concrete Plan

1. Run 01_google_smoke.py once fully (kill Chrome stale lockfiles first: rm ~/.searxng-mcp/browser-session-smoke/Singleton*)
2. Review the smoke_<timestamp>.md report with user
3. If Google <30/30: diagnose per status (CAPTCHA / BLOCKED / EMPTY / SUSPECT) — likely needs WebGL fingerprint patch, consent-banner fix, or shorter delay calibration
4. Iterate until Google 30/30 clean
5. THEN: expand to bing (parser issue?), scholar (0-results root cause?)
6. THEN: run tier-ranking eval via 15_tier_ranking_eval.py (code ready in commit 2a38122)

- Decisions-files cleanup (search01/03/04 are stale but touching them before eval is pointless)
- Brave / mojeek / startpage / ddg re-enablement (dropped, out of scope for engine reliability work)

description:
## What

Migration der heute in dev/search_pipeline/14-16 empirisch validierten PDF-Download-Chain in src/. Aktueller Status der Probes:
- 14_download_classify_probe.py — 732 URLs sniff-classified, Tier-1 Transforms 100% Erfolg
- 15_citation_pdf_followup.py — 124 HTML_HAS_PDF_LINK URLs probed, 71/124 = 57% PDF_OK via Multi-Step
- 16_search_to_pdf_probe.py — End-to-End: 3 Queries → 10 PDFs in 28s in ~/Downloads

## Was migriert wird (alle empirisch belegt)

### A) Tier-1 URL Transforms

Neues Modul (z.B. `src/scraper/pdf_chain.py`) mit TRANSFORM_MAP:

- arxiv.org: `re.sub(r'^/(abs|html)/', '/pdf/', path)` — deckt /abs/, /html/, mit/ohne Versions-Suffix
- aclanthology.org: trailing-slash stripped + .pdf suffix (skip if already .pdf)
- openreview.net: /forum?id=X → /pdf?id=X

Alle drei in der End-to-End-Probe 100% Erfolgsquote.

### B) Multi-Step citation_pdf_url

Wenn Domain weder Tier-1 noch Hard-Blacklist und URL nicht .pdf-Suffix:
1. GET HTML, extract `<meta name='citation_pdf_url' content='...'>` (regex deckt beide Attribut-Reihenfolgen)
2. Wenn gefunden: GET die extrahierte URL
3. Wenn Hop-2 = PDF (Content-Type oder %PDF-Magic): success
4. Sonst: HTML_FALLBACK = silent paywall (kein PDF erreichbar)

Per-source-domain Erfolgsquote aus Probe 15:
- mdpi.com 100% (2/2), proceedings.scipy.org 100% (8/8), journals.plos.org 100% (5/5)
- doi.org 64% (68/107) — wenn der Resolver auf OA-Verlag landet
- link.springer.com 0/11 als Source (book chapters paywalled), aber 13/48 als PDF-Host (OA journal articles funktionieren)

### C) Hard-Blacklist

Domains die deterministisch HTML_OK ohne citation_pdf_url liefern (kein Recovery-Pfad). Aus Probe 14 verifiziert:
- books.google.com (preview-only, 59/59 HTML_OK)
- jstor.org (4/4)
- ieeexplore.ieee.org (6/6)
- muse.jhu.edu (4/4)
- search.proquest.com (4/4)
- scribd.com (3/3 — Vorsicht: scribd hat manchmal Book-Previews die wir für --books wollen)
- nature.com (2/2 — nicht 100% sicher, könnte OA-Nature-Artikel mit citation_pdf_url geben; im Doubt drin lassen)
- search.ebscohost.com
- spiedigitallibrary.org
- semanticscholar.org (Tier-2, brauchte engine-fix; bis dahin blacklist)
- openalex.org (Tier-2, brauchte engine-fix; bis dahin blacklist)

### D) GitHub-Blob-Exclusion

github.com/{owner}/{repo}/blob/{branch}/{path}.pdf — Pfad endet auf .pdf aber Server liefert HTML (Webview). In End-to-End-Probe 16 zwei FAILs durch genau dieses Muster.

Lösung: github.com/*/blob/* explizit als 'never PDF' markieren. github.com/*/raw/* und raw.githubusercontent.com bleiben erlaubt falls in Suchergebnissen.

## Where

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| src/scraper/pdf_chain.py (NEW) | TRANSFORM_MAP, BLACKLIST set, GITHUB_BLOB_PATTERN, citation_pdf_url extraction, async two-hop helper |
| src/scraper/download_pdf.py | Refactor download_pdf_workflow um Chain zu nutzen statt nur .endswith('.pdf') |
| cli.py (Z. 154/162) | Auto-Routing-Logik in scrape_url + scrape_url_raw: nicht mehr nur .endswith('.pdf') sondern via Chain entscheiden |
| dev/search_pipeline/16_search_to_pdf_probe.py | Refactor: importiert jetzt aus src/scraper/pdf_chain statt inline-Logik. Bleibt als Validations-Tool. |

## Verification

- src/scraper/pdf_chain.py hat unit-tests für jeden Transform (arxiv abs/html/version-suffix, acl trailing-slash, openreview)
- src/scraper/pdf_chain.py hat Test für GitHub-blob → markiert als nicht-downloadbar
- 16_search_to_pdf_probe.py nach Refactor liefert die gleichen 10 PDFs für die drei Test-Queries (regression check)
- Manueller cli.py Test: searxng-cli scrape_url 'https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.13277' → soll PDF in Downloads landen statt HTML

## Approach

Plan-Pflicht (multi-file Refactor, design choices um pdf_chain.py-Interface). Worker bekommt diese Description, schlägt Phase A vor mit Modul-Aufteilung, Test-Coverage, Refactor-Strategie für probe 16.

## Out of Scope

- Tier-2 Engine-Level-Fix (OpenAlex oa_url, SemanticScholar openAccessPdf) — eigener Bead, nicht jetzt
- doi.org Multi-Step-Resolution mit Sub-Flag — dieser Bead nutzt nur die EXISTING Hop-2-via-citation_pdf_url, kein zusätzlicher resolver
- --pdf Flag und Whitelist-Filterung im Search-Pipeline — gehört zu searxng-x4f
- DOCS.md Updates für src/scraper/ — nach Migration zu erledigen

## Bezüge

- Probe-Skripte: dev/search_pipeline/14-16 + Reports in 01_reports/ (alle heute committed)
- Parallel-Bead: searxng-jqn (Search-Foundation, kein Datei-Konflikt)
- Folge-Bead: searxng-x4f (--pdf flag, hängt an dieser Migration UND an jqn Free-Word-Hook)

description:
## Why

The current scrape pipeline (src/scraper/, src/crawler/) feels too cumbersome for the typical search → scrape → read flow. The complete workflow (search → scrape → crawl → clean → index) is only justified for sources we want in RAG. For a quick relevance check, less heavy weight is needed.

User wishes — two modes:

- **Mode 1**: complete content, manually cleaned, for indexing into RAG
- **Mode 2**: as much clean content as possible without overload, for quick relevance judgment in the normal search/scrape flow

Coverage > cleanliness: occasional noise is acceptable, missing content is not.

User directive: we will NOT build scrapers ourselves. We use libraries.

## Scope

Investigation worker `scrape-prep` dispatched in session 2026-05-05. Three deliverables (worker stops + reports between each):

1. **Status quo (consolidated)** — IST of scraping (one-page) and crawling (multi-page). Reads src/scraper/, src/crawler/, src/routing.py, decisions/scrape01..03 + explore01, src/scraper/DOCS, src/crawler/DOCS, dev/scrape_pipeline/, dev/explore_pipeline/, dev/cleanup/. Known-broken (refs searxng-31z, searxng-9ui), redundancy (explore_site duplication scraper/ vs crawler/).

2. **Library shortlist (LITE)** — github-search skill activated. Named candidates: trafilatura, readability-lxml, goose3, newspaper3k, jusText, dragnet, boilerpipe. Plus 1–2 broader queries for LLM-era tools (Apify SDK, scrapy-playwright, Reader-LM, etc.). Output table: stars, last activity, scope, Mode 1 fit, Mode 2 fit, verdict. NO source-dive into single libraries.

3. **Hands-on observations** — depends on dl9 smoke being ready (dev/search_pipeline/01_reports/pipeline_smoke_*.md). Pick 10 URLs (4 GENERAL, 3 ACADEMIC, 2 QA, 1 wildcard), scrape via current scrape_url, table outcomes (success, bytes, content quality, garbage detection, truncation).

Worker commits final consolidated report to dev/scrape_pipeline/01_redesign_prep_<date>.md. NO src/ edits.

## Mode 1 / Mode 2 design

NOT in this worker's scope. Design = Opus + User discussion AFTER the three reports. Then dev scaffolding (likely a new dev/scrape_pipeline/mode_eval/ sub-suite) for prototyping mode logic before prod migration — same pattern as src/search/ vs dev/search_pipeline/.

## Dependencies

- Step 3 depends on dl9 smoke output
- Related open beads: searxng-31z (scrape_url failures), searxng-9ui (scrape-pipe refactor + crawler/explore SOLL state) — both should be cross-referenced in IST report; this redesign likely subsumes them
